title-group.article-title: Reduced PRC2 function causes asparaginase resistance in T-ALL by decreasing WNT pathway activity∗

contrib-group.aff: 1Systems Biology Ireland, University College Dublin, Dublin, Ireland
2School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Dublin, Ireland
3Research Ireland Centre for Research Training in Genomics Data Science, Galway, Ireland
4Department of Haematology, UCL Cancer Institute, University College London, London, United Kingdom
5University of Dublin, Trinity College, Dublin, Ireland
6National Children’s Cancer Service, Children’s Health Ireland at Crumlin, Dublin, Ireland
7Division of Developmental Biology and Cancer, Great Ormond Street Institute of Child Health, London, United Kingdom
8Conway Institute, University College Dublin, Dublin, Ireland

author-notes.corresp: ∗Correspondence: Jonathan Bond, Systems Biology Ireland, School of Medicine, University College Dublin, Belfield, Dublin 4, Ireland; jonathan.bond@ucd.ie

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

list-item.p: Reduced PRC2 function in T-ALL is associated with asparaginase resistance that is linked to reduced WNT/STOP pathway activity.
Asparaginase resistance in these cases can be mitigated by coadministration of a proteasome inhibitor.

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Loss-of-function mutations and deletions in the core components of the epigenetic polycomb repressive complex 2 (PRC2) are associated with poor initial treatment response in T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (T-ALL), but the mechanisms that underpin resistance to individual therapies are unknown. We leveraged an isogenic T-ALL cellular model and primary patient data to investigate how PRC2 alterations affect signaling pathway activity in leukemia cells, and whether these changes may influence therapy response. The integration of transcriptomic, proteomic, and phosphoproteomic results revealed markedly reduced activity of the WNT-dependent stabilization of proteins (WNT/STOP) pathway in leukemia cells lacking core PRC2 factor EZH2. Importantly, these results closely matched transcriptional readouts from the samples of patients with T-ALL with PRC2 mutations and deletions. We discovered that PRC2 loss significantly reduced sensitivity to key T-ALL treatment asparaginase, and that this was mechanistically linked to increased cellular ubiquitination levels due to WNT/STOP suppression, which bolstered the asparagine reserves of leukemia cells. These results also strongly correlated with transcriptional profiles of asparaginase resistance in an independent cohort of patients with T-ALL. We further found that asparaginase resistance in PRC2-depleted leukemic blasts could be mitigated by pharmaceutical proteasome inhibition, thereby providing a potential avenue to tackle induction treatment failure in these cases.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Outcomes for young patients with T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (T-ALL) have lagged behind B-cell ALL in the same age group,1 in part due to a less complete mechanistic understanding of the molecular drivers of treatment resistance.

Recent large-scale genomic efforts have highlighted a key role for epigenetic factors in aggressive T-ALL biology, as shown by the high frequency (c.40%) of loss-of-function mutations and deletions in genes coding for polycomb repressive complex 2 (PRC2) factors in the immature T-ALL subgroup.2, 3, 4, 5 In line with the elevated rates of remission induction failure in these leukemias,6 reduced PRC2 function has been shown to correlate with chemotherapy resistance in both T-ALL and acute myeloid leukemias that harbor similar mutations in the core PRC2 components EZH2, EED, and SUZ12.2,7,8

Various molecular explanations have been proposed to explain chemoresistance in PRC2-deficient T-ALL, including changes in NOTCH1-mediated transcriptional regulation9 and increased mitochondrial apoptosis resistance,10 but a clear mechanistic link between PRC2 loss of function and altered sensitivity to any specific T-ALL remission induction treatment has not yet been established.

Mutational co-occurrence may provide clues to the mechanistic basis of this therapy resistance. Epigenetic alterations in T-ALL frequently segregate with activating mutations in signaling molecules, suggesting that these alterations interact functionally.3,4,11,12 Signaling pathways are appealing treatment targets due to their therapeutic tractability, and there is evidence that kinase inhibition can be leveraged to improve drug responses in combination with epigenetic agents13 and in leukemias that harbor epigenetic factor alterations.14

Recent work has also revealed important roles for signaling pathways that are comparatively less studied in T-ALL biology. In particular, several reports have elegantly identified that therapeutic responses to the key T-ALL remission induction treatment asparaginase are heavily influenced by the activity level of the WNT-dependent stabilization of proteins (WNT/STOP) pathway, which regulates the asparagine reserves of leukemic blasts.15, 16, 17

In this study, we leveraged clinical mutational and transcriptional data and cellular models of leukemia PRC2 loss to explore the functional interplay between epigenetic factor and signaling pathway alterations in T-ALL, with an aim of understanding how these interactions alter responses to specific therapies. We notably find that PRC2-depleted cells have decreased WNT/STOP activity that correlates with reduced asparaginase sensitivity and that this asparaginase resistance can be mitigated by targeting the ubiquitination-proteasomal axis pharmaceutically.

sec.title: CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing
sec.p: Two guide RNAs (gRNAs; gRNA1: CGGAAATCTTAAACCAAGAATG, gRNA2: ACCAAGAATGGAAACAGCGAAG) were designed to direct the Cas9 endonuclease to exon 3 of EZH2. gRNAs were purchased from Integrated DNA Technologies as part of the Alt-R CRISPR-Cas9 System, which delivers the ribonucleoprotein complex comprising gRNA, tracrRNA, and Cas9 enzyme. Jurkat cells were transfected using the Cell Line Nucleofector Kit V (VCA-1003; Lonza) coupled with the Amaxa Nucleofector System (Nucleofector 2b; Lonza).

sec.title: RNA-seq
sec.p: RNA was extracted using the RNAeasy kit (Qiagen). PolyA-enriched paired-end sequencing was performed using the Illumina NovaSeq 6000 Sequencing System at a depth of 30 million reads. Workflows for preprocessing and analyzing RNA-sequencing (RNA-seq) data to generate gene counts were derived from https://diytranscriptomics.com/ and are available at https://github.com/cosmintudose/RNASeq_Jurkat_pipeline. PROGENy (Pathway RespOnsive GENes) was performed according to the original description of the pipeline18 available at https://github.com/saezlab/transcriptutorial. Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) using the H, C2, and C6 sets was performed using the GSEABase 1.56.0 and msigdbr 7.4.1 R packages.

sec.title: Proteomic analysis
sec.p: Mass spectrometry (MS) output data were searched using MaxQuant (release 2.0.1.0) against the Homo sapiens subset of the UniProt Swiss-Prot database with specific parameters for trapped ion mobility spectrometry data–dependent acquisition. The MaxQuant output file was imported into the Perseus (version 1.6.15.0) environment for protein quantification. Phosphoproteome data were similarly exported and transformed to enable analysis of phosphorylation level and residue position. Exported data were loaded into the R environment (R version 4.0.4, libraries: dplyr 1.0.8, gtools 3.9.2) for further analysis. Kinase-substrate enrichment analysis (KSEA) was performed as previously described.19

Full methods for cell culture, assessment of EZH2-deleted Jurkat clones, MS, immunoblotting, drug treatments, cell viability testing, RNA-seq data analysis, and KSEA are provided in the supplemental Methods. For the reviewers’ attention, the main supplemental Data comprises supplemental Methods, supplemental Table 4, supplemental Figures 1-4, and supplemental References.

sec.title: Loss of PRC2 function correlates with reduced transcription of WNT pathway targets
sec.p:
To study the effects of PRC2 loss of function on signaling pathway activity in T-ALL, we created an isogenic Jurkat cell line model using CRISPR/Cas9 to target exon 3 of EZH2 (Figure 1A; supplemental Figure 1A). Notably, Jurkat wild-type (WT) cells do not harbor any genetic alterations of known clinical significance in PRC2 factors or lysine 27 of histone H3 (H3K27) demethylase enzymes.Figure 1.
Loss of PRC2 function correlates with reduced transcription of WNT pathway targets. (A) Schema of isogenic cell line model and (B) confirmation of reduced EZH2 and H3K27me3 in EZH2-KO clones A7 and C9 by protein immunoblotting. (C) PROGENy analysis of signaling pathway activity from RNA-seq transcriptional readouts. Enrichment in EZH2-KO cells is depicted, that is, negative enrichment corresponds to the predicted decreased activity of that pathway in PRC2-depleted cells. Supplemental Figure 1D displays the comparison of individual clones with WT samples. (D) Altered transcription of WNT pathway targets in PRC2-depleted T-ALL. Genes with statistically significant gene expression changes between WT and EZH2-KO Jurkat cell lines are shown. GSEAs of WNT pathway–related signatures in EZH2 WT and KO Jurkat cells (E) and PRC2-WT and altered primary T-ALLs (F) reveal similar transcriptional patterns. Gene sets are indicated above each panel. In panels E-F, the genes downregulated following deletion of β-catenin (CTNNB1) are also downregulated in PRC2-depleted cell lines and PRC2-altered patient samples (left); and positive enrichment is observed for genes downregulated by the WNT3A-enriched microenvironment (middle). In contrast, in panels E-F (right) genes upregulated by the WNT3A-enriched microenvironment Frizzled are negatively enriched in the context of PRC2 depletion or alteration Supplemental Figure 1F and supplemental Table 1 provide the full GSEA results, with the pathways shown in Figure 1E-F highlighted in bold in supplemental Table 1. EGFR, epidermal growth factor receptor; H3K27me3, lysine 27 of histone H3 trimethylation; NES, normalized enrichment score; PI3K, phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase; TGF-β, transforming growth factor β; TNF-α; tumor necrosis factor α; VEGF, vascular endothelial growth factor.

Considering that CRISPR/Cas9-knockout (KO) clones are known to harbor cryptic genetic and functional heterogeneity,20 we included an additional EZH2-KO clone that was previously generated from a different parental Jurkat line using an alternative gene-editing strategy21 in our analyses to increase the likelihood that any molecular changes we detected were specifically related to PRC2 loss, rather than random clonal phenotypic variability.

We confirmed reductions of EZH2 protein expression and function by measurement of trimethylation of lysine 27 of histone H3 in these clones by immunoblotting (Figure 1B) before performing comprehensive molecular characterization of both lines.

We reasoned that transcriptional readouts would provide the best starting point for assessing changes in cellular signaling activity following EZH2 loss, and therefore prioritized these analyses. We performed RNA-seq of WT and EZH2-deficient lines and used PROGENy18 to analyze the results. PROGENy uses curated gene expression and perturbation data to infer the activity of 14 separate signaling cascades, thereby helping us to assess how the function of these pathways differed between WT and EZH2-depleted cells. Given that the 2 EZH2-KO clones presented comparable gene expression profiles (supplemental Figure 1B), these transcriptional results were combined and compared with WT readouts for the analysis.

PROGENy revealed altered activity of multiple signaling pathways in EZH2-null cells (Figure 1C; supplemental Figure 1C), including epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), transforming growth factor β (both increased), MAPK, and hypoxia (both decreased). We were particularly struck by the differences in inferred WNT pathway activity between WT and EZH2-deficient cells. WNT was found to be the most downregulated signaling cascade in EZH2-KO cells, with marked transcriptional changes in targets of both canonical and noncanonical WNT pathways (Figure 1D; supplemental Figure 1D).

To confirm that these gene expression changes were not specific to our cell line model, we analyzed a cohort of patients with T-ALL3 using GSEA to determine the transcriptional differences between PRC2-WT and PRC2-altered leukemias (cohort details are in supplemental Figure 1E). The initial review of the most significantly altered oncogenic signature (C6) gene sets from Molecular Signatures Database (MSigDB) confirmed marked negative enrichment for genes previously described to decrease in expression following either EZH2 or embryonic ectoderm development (EED) knockdown in samples with PRC2 loss, providing support for use of this method to determine transcriptional differences between WT and PRC2-altered primary samples (supplemental Figure 1F).

Notably, several sets of genes related to WNT pathway activity were also among the most enriched gene sets in PRC2-altered samples in this unbiased analysis (full results in supplemental Table 1), including WNT3A/Frizzled, β-catenin, and LEF1 targets. In each case, transcriptional differences between PRC2-altered and PRC2-WT samples from patients with T-ALL corresponded with those observed between WT and EZH2-null Jurkat cells (Figure 1E-F; supplemental Figure 1F).

All these results provide transcriptional evidence of decreased WNT activity in primary leukemias with altered PRC2 function and suggest that the molecular changes in the Jurkat EZH2-KO model can be extrapolated to the PRC2-depleted T-ALL molecular landscape in vivo.

sec.title: Multiomics analysis confirms reduced WNT signaling activity in PRC2-depleted T-ALL
sec.p: We next determined whether the transcriptional changes associated with PRC2 loss correlated with proteomic and phosphoproteomic assessment of signaling pathway activity.

MS analysis of whole-cell extracts revealed significant differences in the proteomes of WT and EZH2-deficient cells (Figure 2A). Importantly, there was significant overlap in the repertoire of proteins that were significantly upregulated and downregulated in each EZH2-KO clone compared with that in the WT sample (supplemental Figure 2A).Figure 2.
Multiomics analysis reveals reduced WNT signaling activity in PRC2-depleted T-ALL. Volcano plots of whole-cell proteomic (A) and phosphoproteomic (B) analysis of extracts from WT and EZH2-deficient cells. WNT pathways factors are highlighted in each case. (C) KSEA18 of phosphoproteomic outputs from WT and EZH2-KO cells. The kinases with relevance for WNT pathway activity are highlighted in bold. The colored bars indicate statistical significance. Supplemental Figure 2B illustrates the comparison of individual EZH2-KO clones with WT samples. (D) Results of the analysis of WNT pathway activity using the Kinase Library tool (https://kinase-library.phosphosite.org/kinase-library/). (E) Protein immunoblotting and quantification of WNT pathway targets. GAPDH, glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase.

Given the transcriptional results that suggested the downregulation of WNT activity in isogenic EZH2-KO cells, we specifically examined proteomic changes in this pathway in our results. A total of 398 WNT-related proteins were identified in our analyses, of which 61 were significantly differently expressed in either WT or EZH2-KO cells (see supplemental Table 2 for full results). Most of these (38/61) were downregulated in both clones, with 18 of 61 commonly upregulated, and only 5 of 61 proteins showed discordant changes between KO clones. WNT factors and targets that changed in expression between WT and KO cells are highlighted in Figure 2A. These included PRKCA and RPS6KA1, which are known to modulate the phosphorylation of negative WNT regulators glycogen synthase kinase 3α (GSK3α) and GSK3β, therby downregulating WNT pathway signaling.

Phosphoproteomic profiling also revealed significant differences between WT and EZH2-null cells, including changes in WNT pathway–related factors, as highlighted in Figure 2B. To gain further insights into these results, we used the KSEA19 tool that assigns a kinase activity score based on the phosphorylation status of known substrates using phospho-site–specific kinase-substrate databases. The full details of these outputs are available in supplemental Table 3 and are summarized in Figure 2C, with individual comparisons for each EZH2 clone shown in supplemental Figure 2B. In line with our results thus far, we found that significant KSEA changes included several kinases that are known to modulate WNT pathway activity. In particular, kinases that are known to inhibit WNT signaling (eg, GSK3A, DAPK3, MAPK9, and CSNK1D) were found to have statistically significant increased activity by KSEA, whereas kinases that have been described to stimulate WNT (eg, PRKCE and MAPK13) had significantly lower KSEA scores. Further analysis using the Kinase Library tool22 showed similar patterns of positive and negative enrichment for these WNT-related kinase pathways (Figure 2D).

Immunoblotting results also reflected decreased WNT activity in EZH2-depleted lines and were in line with proteomic and phosphoproteomic outputs, with the protein levels of WNT transcriptional targets being reduced in EZH2-null cells compared with their isogenic WT equivalents (Figure 2E). Immunoblotting for WNT pathway members is shown in supplemental Figure 2C.

sec.title: Asparaginase resistance in PRC2-deficient T-ALL correlates with reduced WNT/STOP pathway activity
sec.p: Having established that PRC2 loss is associated with reduced WNT pathway activity both in vitro and in patient samples, and in light of the previously reported links between PRC2 mutations and deletions and chemoresistance in acute leukemia,7, 8, 9, 10 we next tested whether these signaling changes had any implications for T-ALL response to treatment.

We first assessed the responses of WT and PRC2-depleted Jurkat cells to a range of standard leukemia therapies, including all agents currently used in the remission induction phase of T-ALL treatment. Our results were consistent with known drug sensitivities of the Jurkat cell line,23 with no observed differences in response to dexamethasone, daunorubicin, vincristine, or cytarabine between WT and EZH2-deleted cells (supplemental Figure 3A). In contrast, we found a marked difference in sensitivity to asparaginase, with EZH2-null Jurkat cells being significantly more resistant to killing than their WT equivalents (P < .001 in each case [Figure 3A], with all experimental replicates shown in supplemental Figure 3B). Notably, EZH2 complementation caused the abrogation of this resistance that correlated with EZH2 protein expression levels, indicating a dosage effect for EZH2 in this context (supplemental Figure 3C). The treatment of the CCRF-CEM and DND41 cell lines with the EZH2 PROTAC MS8847 also caused decreased asparaginase sensitivity, supporting the role of EZH2 in mediating asparaginase responses independently of T-ALL genetic background (Figure 3B; supplemental Figure 3D).Figure 3.
Asparaginase resistance in PRC2-deficient T-ALL correlates with reduced WNT/STOP pathway activity. (A) Differences in the asparaginase sensitivity of WT and EZH2-deficient Jurkat cells. A representative experiment is shown, with full results of all experimental replicates provided in supplemental Figure 3B. Stars indicate statistical significance (P < .001). (B) Measurement of asparaginase sensitivity in CCRF-CEM (left) and DND41 (right) T-ALL cell lines in the presence or absence of treatment with the EZH2 PROTAC MS8847. Confirmation of PROTAC activity is shown in supplemental Figure 3D. (C) GSEAs comparing transcriptional profiles from patient samples that underwent ex vivo drug sensitivity testing.24 Asparaginase-resistant leukemias (supplemental Figure 3C) show positive enrichment for genes that are upregulated in PRC2-mutated/deleted T-ALLs from an independent cohort3 (top right panel) and for those associated with EZH2 loss in the Jurkat cell line model reported here (top left panel). The bottom panels show the converse negative enrichment for downregulated genes in primary samples and the cell line model. (D) Measurement of ubiquitination levels by immunoblotting in (left to right) WT and EZH2-deficient Jurkat cells and other T-ALL cell lines (Jurkat, DND-41, and CCRF-CEM) treated for 48 hours with either enzymatic inhibition of EZH2 (GSK343) or a PROTAC EED degrader (UNC6852), as indicated. (E) Measurement of ubiquitination levels in T-ALL cell lines after treatment with either GSK343, UNC6852, a GSK3 inhibitor (BI-5521), or a WNT agonist (WA1). EZH2i, EZH2 inhibitor GSK343; FDR, false discovery rate; NES, normalized enrichment score.

To test whether asparaginase response could be linked to PRC2 loss of function in patient samples, we leveraged an analysis of ex vivo drug sensitivity in primary ALLs24 and compared the transcriptional profiles of asparaginase-sensitive and asparaginase-resistant leukemias (supplemental Figure 3E). GSEA analyses revealed that asparaginase-resistant samples had positive enrichment for genes upregulated in PRC2-altered T-ALLs from an independent cohort of patients with T-ALL3 and from our isogenic EZH2-KO cell line model (Figure 3C). Therefore, these results point toward a common transcriptional signature between asparaginase sensitivity and PRC2 loss of function in leukemic cells.

Recent reports have demonstrated a strong link between WNT pathway activity and asparaginase sensitivity in both acute leukemia and colorectal cancer15, 16, 17 and led us to investigate whether this mechanism was responsible for asparaginase resistance in PRC2-depleted T-ALL. This described mechanism of resistance is caused by the altered activity of the WNT/STOP pathway,25,26 which normally inhibits GSK3-dependent protein ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation. Reduced WNT/STOP activity leads to increased protein ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation, thereby providing a catabolic source of amino acids that allows cancer cells to circumvent the asparagine depletion normally induced by asparaginase.

To explore this mechanism, we first measured protein ubiquitination in the Jurkat cell line model and found elevated levels of ubiquitination in EZH2-null cells (Figure 3D). We further confirmed that reducing PRC2 activity by either chemical EZH2 inhibition or EED protein depletion using a PROTAC degrader caused similar significant increases in protein ubiquitination in WT Jurkat and other T-ALL cell lines (Figure 3D, with confirmation of the drug effect shown in supplemental Figure 3F), whereas the pharmaceutical stimulation of the WNT pathway with either a GSK3 stabilizer or WNT agonist caused decreased ubiquitination in T-ALL cells (Figure 3E). Notably, asparagine synthetase (ASNS) levels were reduced in EZH2-null clones, whereas ASNS protein showed either equivalent or reduced stability, suggesting that changes in ASNS activity did not contribute to asparaginase resistance in these cells (supplemental Figure 3G).

sec.title: Asparaginase resistance in PRC2-deficient T-ALL can be mitigated by inhibiting proteasome activity
sec.p: All our results suggest that PRC2 loss of function reduces asparaginase sensitivity in T-ALL cells by decreasing WNT/STOP pathway activity. To further test this mechanism and to determine whether it may be possible to improve asparaginase sensitivity in PRC2-deficient cells, we used drug treatments to modulate the WNT/STOP–ubiquitination–proteasome axis.

We first tried to stimulate WNT pathway signaling using either a GSK3 stabilizer or a WNT agonist, but found that these agents caused significant toxicity in T-ALL cell lines, which precluded assessing their effectiveness in combination with asparaginase (supplemental Figure 4A).

We then hypothesized that treatment with a proteasome inhibitor would counteract the asparaginase resistance observed in PRC2-deficient cells, because this would reduce flux through the proteasome and thereby deplete the catabolic source of asparagine provided by increased protein degradation due to reduced WNT/STOP pathway activity. Considering that proteasome inhibition is toxic to T-ALL cells, we used an extremely low dose of the proteasome inhibitor bortezomib (1nM), which did not cause cell death when administered as a single agent (supplemental Figure 4B) in our combination experiments.

In line with our hypothesis, we found that bortezomib treatment mitigated asparaginase resistance in EZH2-null Jurkat cells, in each case at least partially restoring sensitivity to the levels observed in the WT line (Figure 4A; supplemental Figure 4C). To further confirm that this effect was linked to ubiquitination, we coadministered the ubiquitination inhibitor TAK-243 with asparaginase in separate experiments and found that this combination treatment also increased sensitivity of EZH2-null Jurkat cells to levels similar to WT responses (Figure 4B; supplemental Figure 4C-D). We also found that the coadministration of bortezomib increased in vitro asparaginase sensitivity of a xenograft sample derived from a patient with ETP-ALL who carried an inactivating mutation in the PRC2 component EED21,27 in a dose-dependent manner (supplemental Figure 4E). Therefore, these results confirm a direct role for proteasomal activity in this resistance mechanism and provide a potential avenue toward increase asparaginase sensitivity in vivo. A summary of this proposed mechanism is shown in Figure 4C.Figure 4.
Asparaginase resistance in PRC2-deficient T-ALL can be mitigated by inhibiting proteasome activity. (A) Comparison of single-agent asparaginase treatment and combination with the proteasome inhibitor bortezomib in WT and EZH2-KO Jurkat cell lines. Bortezomib was administered 8 hours before asparaginase treatment, with viability readouts at 48 hours postasparaginase dose. (B) Comparison of single-agent asparaginase treatment and combination with the ubiquitination inhibitor TAK243 in WT and EZH2-KO Jurkat cell lines. Representative experiments are shown, with the full results of all experimental replicates provided in supplemental Figure 4C. (C) Proposed mechanism for asparaginase resistance and mitigation by proteasomal and ubiquitination inhibition in PRC2-deficient T-ALL. Ub, ubiquitination.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: We provide a holistic analysis of signaling pathway changes in PRC2-altered T-ALL, identify a mechanistic explanation of resistance to a key blood cancer treatment, and suggest potential solutions to overcome this clinical problem.

Asparaginase is a central plank of leukemia treatment during the initial phase of remission induction therapy, and suboptimal asparaginase responses due to intrinsic cellular resistance or acquired immune sensitivities are strongly linked to poor outcomes.28, 29, 30 However, why some T-ALLs fail to respond to this agent is not fully clear. Sensitivity is at least partly mediated by the levels of ASNS expression31,32 and epigenetic modification of ASNS transcription,33 but these relationships are inconsistent.34, 35, 36 In line with this, EZH2-depleted cells in our experiments had decreased levels of ASNS, which would be predicted to cause increased asparaginase sensitivity, but we found that the opposite was the case.

Recent insights into asparaginase resistance mechanisms were provided by several elegant reports that linked WNT/STOP pathway activity to asparaginase sensitivity in leukemia and colorectal cancer.15, 16, 17 Our results add to this knowledge by providing a novel link between asparaginase resistance by this mechanism and a genetic alteration in T-ALL cells, that is, reduced polycomb function through either mutation or deletion of core PRC2 factors. To our knowledge, this is the first description of a mechanistic link between a somatic genetic alteration and response to asparaginase treatment in T-ALL.

One limitation of our study was the cellular context in which much of the molecular analysis of signaling pathway activity was conducted. Whereas most PRC2 mutations and deletions in patient samples occur in the immature/ETP-ALL subgroup,2,3 the cell lines used in this study correspond to the later stage of differentiation arrest. Nevertheless, our results were reproducible in 3 different T-ALL lines with different oncogenetic backgrounds, supporting the idea that the effect of EZH2 loss is consistent in different T-lymphoblastic contexts. Another caveat is that the measurement of viability by Alamar Blue in most of our results may not fully reflect the activation of downstream apoptotic pathways in all cases, albeit the patterns we observed are highly consistent with known drug sensitivity patterns in each case.

Despite this caveat, it is important to note that previous analyses showed that increased expression of genes were associated with an immature/ETP-ALL phenotype in this model,21 which would therefore mimic the most frequent transcriptional setting in patient cells. In addition, the identification of a specific molecular vulnerability to CHK1 inhibition in this experimental system was corroborated by results in PRC2-altered primary patient samples, providing support for this model as a reliable proxy for PRC2-altered T-ALL in vivo. Furthermore, our key finding that transcription of WNT pathway genes is reduced in PRC2-altered patient leukemia samples, in line with expression patterns observed in our in vitro model (Figure 1); the fact that the drug targeting of PRC2 markedly increases ubiquitination activity in other cell models (Figure 3); and the key finding that asparaginase sensitivity in primary samples is strongly linked to PRC2-dependent gene signatures (Figure 3) support our confidence that these findings can be extrapolated to other T-ALL contexts.

We have shown that the coadministration of the proteasome inhibitor bortezomib can mitigate asparaginase resistance in PRC2-depleted cells, thereby suggesting a potential avenue toward modulating therapy response in patients in future. Future studies should test and compare these potential drug synergies in primary patient samples and preclinical models with PRC2-WT and PRC2-mutant backgrounds. We note that the choice of dose used in our experiments was carefully titrated to nontoxic levels of bortezomib concentration to avoid any confounding effects of direct bortezomib activity on cell viability. In our experiments, even this extremely small dose of 1nM resulted in improved asparaginase sensitivity in combination treatments. It is highly likely that the use of even moderately higher doses of proteasome inhibitors would result in increased levels of leukemia cell death that could be of greater benefit in the clinical setting.

Given the central role that polycomb complexes play in transcriptional regulation (reviewed by Schuettengruber et al,37 Blackledge et al,38 and Di Croce and Helin39) and the previous reports of EZH2 regulation of WNT signaling in other cellular contexts,40,41 a key question raised by our findings is how PRC2 and other epigenetic factors regulate the expression of genes that modulate the activity of WNT and other signaling pathways in leukemia cells. Although our results provide a profile of the transcriptional changes linked to EZH2 loss due to mutations or deletions, an important next step will be to analyze how PRC2 genetic alterations affect the binding of polycomb and other epigenetic factors across the genome, particularly at WNT-related loci. It will also be critical to evaluate whether other T-ALL somatic genetic changes also alter WNT/STOP activity and whether the coadministration of proteasome inhibitors may provide opportunities to improve asparaginase responses and overall treatment outcomes in other high-risk leukemia subgroups.
Conflict-of-interest disclosure: The authors declare no competing financial interests.

Genome regulation by polycomb and trithorax: 70 years and counting
Targeting Polycomb systems to regulate gene expression: modifications to a complex story
Transcriptional regulation by Polycomb group proteins
Ezh2 promotes mammary tumor initiation through epigenetic regulation of the Wnt and mTORC1 signaling pathways
element-citation.source: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A

element-citation.article-title: Histone H3K27 methyltransferase Ezh2 represses Wnt genes to facilitate adipogenesis
element-citation.source: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A

caption.title: Supplemental Methods, Table 4, Figures, and References

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: The authors acknowledge the many team members at Systems Biology Ireland and the Irish National Children’s Cancer Service at Children’s Health Ireland at Crumlin for their support and helpful suggestions during the course of this study.

The authors thank the National Children’s Research Centre and The Children’s Health Foundation for their significant support of this work through Leadership Award A-18-3, which funded start-up research in the Bond laboratory at Systems Biology Ireland and fully supported T.L.’s doctoral work. The work in the Bond laboratory is also funded by 10.13039/501100001602Research Ireland grants 20/FFP-P/8844 and 18/SPP/3522, the latter together with 10.13039/100012422Children's Health Ireland. The work in the Ryan laboratory is supported by 10.13039/501100001602Research Ireland grant 20/FFP-P/8641. C.T. was funded by 10.13039/501100001602Research Ireland through the SFI Centre for Research Training in Genomics Data Science under grant number 18/CRT/6214 and supported in part by the European Union’s 10.13039/501100007601Horizon 2020 research and innovation program under the Marie Skłodowska-Curie grant H2020-MSCA-COFUND-2019-945385. Proteomic and phosphoproteomic analyses were supported by the Comprehensive Molecular Analytical Platform under the SFI Research Infrastructure Programme (reference 18/RI/5702).

sec.title: Authorship
sec.p: Contribution: T.L., C.J.R., and J.B. contributed to conceptualization; T.L., T.-I.G., C.J.R., and J.B. contributed to methodology and validation; T.L., T.-I.G., C.T., L.J., C.J.R., and J.B. contributed to formal analysis; T.L., T.I.-G., C.T., L.J., T.E.L., K.W., and G.O. contributed to investigation; T.E.L. and M.R.M. contributed to resources; T.L., C.T., T.-I.G., K.W., and G.O. contributed to data curation; T.L. and J.B. contributed to writing the original draft; T.L., T.-I.G., C.T., C.J.R., and J.B. contributed to visualization; C.J.R. and J.B. contributed to supervision, project administration, and funding acquisition; and all authors contributed to the review and editing of the manuscript.

fn.p: T.L. and T.-I.G. contributed equally to this study.

The RNA-sequencing data have been deposited in the Gene Expression Omnibus database (accession number GSE240152). Mass spectrometry proteomics data have been deposited to the ProteomeXchange Consortium via the PRoteomics IDEntifications Database partner repository (data set identifier PXD045010).
The full-text version of this article contains a data supplement.
